_meta.verified: each repro re-run through VM + python3
_meta.fixed: 47 bugs fixed and migrated to vm.json (10 samples each)

bugs.summary: any() does not short-circuit, so a walrus var assigned inside the generator keeps the last-iteration value instead of stopping at the matching element.
bugs.repro:
def foo():
    any((hit := i) == 8 for i in range(20))
    return hit
print(foo())

bugs.summary: `continue` inside a for-else clause that targets the enclosing outer loop sends the VM into an infinite loop (budget exceeded) instead of continuing the outer loop.
An exception raised in a try passes through an inner finally but then fails to propagate to the outer except handler; the VM swallows it.
A break in an inner try does not execute the enclosing finally body (statements before its own break are skipped).
A continue in a finally does not run, and a return in a nested try skips the enclosing finally entirely; the VM returns 42 instead of looping with continue.
A break inside a try does not execute the enclosing finally block before exiting the loop.
A return inside a try does not execute the enclosing finally block at all; the finally is skipped.
With nested finally blocks, an enclosing finally is not executed during an unwinding return (the outer finally's print is skipped).
An enclosing finally is not run while an unwinding return passes through it, so the finally body output is missing.
A return in an inner finally is not overridden by an enclosing finally's return, and the enclosing finally body does not run.
When an exception propagates through a try/finally nested inside a with block, the VM passes None to __exit__ instead of the exception, losing exception info and breaking suppression semantics.
__exit__ is not called when a break statement exits a with block, so the context manager is not cleaned up.
__exit__ is not called when a continue statement exits a with block, so the context manager is not cleaned up on that iteration.
__exit__ is not called when a return statement exits a with block, so the context manager is not cleaned up.
The '_' grouping separator in a str.format spec ('{:0_x}') is rejected with 'trailing characters in format spec', even though the ',' separator works; valid format spec wrongly errors.
bugs.repro: print("{:0_x}".format(0x1000))
bugs.actual: __VMERR__ ValueError: trailing characters in format spec

bugs.summary: Parser rejects a chained attribute assignment target like a.b.c = 1 (exit 4), which is valid Python using only attribute assignment.
bugs.repro:
class A:
    pass
a = A()
a.b = A()
a.b.c = 1
print(a.b.c)

bugs.summary: del of a subscript on a bytes literal raises a bogus NameError naming the literal instead of TypeError.
A dict or set display as the first element of an unparenthesized tuple fails to parse, though tuples/dicts/sets are all supported.
bugs.repro:
x = {1: 2}, 3
print(x)

bugs.summary: Deleting an attribute (del obj.attr) fails to parse even though attribute access/assignment and del of a name are supported.
bugs.repro:
class C:
    pass
c = C()
c.x = 1
del c.x
print('ok')

bugs.summary: Parser rejects a parenthesized target tuple in a del statement (del (a, b)), which is valid Python; unparenthesized del a, b works.
bugs.repro:
a = 1
b = 2
del (a, b)
print("ok")

bugs.summary: Parser rejects an unparenthesized tuple as the for-loop iterable (for d in {}, {42:2}:); parenthesizing it works. dict.get itself works fine.
bugs.repro:
for x in 1, 2:
    print(x)

bugs.summary: Same parser defect as dict_get: unparenthesized tuple as for-loop iterable (for m in d.items, d.values, d.keys:) is rejected; parenthesized form works.
The file fails at parse time (exit 4) on `for i in s, t:` — a parenthesis-less tuple as the for-loop iterable is rejected, though `for i in (s, t):` works.
bugs.repro:
for i in 1, 2:
    print(i)

bugs.summary: 'yield from' used as an expression whose value is consumed (print((yield from gen()))) is rejected at parse time, though yield from as a statement works and uses only supported features.
'yield from' used as an expression (ret = yield from MyIter()) is rejected at parse time even though that construct uses only supported features; the parser bug blocks the whole file.
Uses only supported features (yield from, exception propagation) but 'yield from' as an expression value is rejected at parse time.
Uses only supported features but bare 'yield' (no value) and 'yield from' as an expression value are rejected at parse time.
A list as an assignment target ([a, b] = ...) is rejected at parse time though it is valid Python using only supported features.
Same defect: 'for i in s, t:' with a bare tuple iterable is rejected at parse time; the set .copy() itself works.
Parser rejects a tuple literal inside an f-string replacement field (a valid, supported f-string feature).
bugs.repro: print(f"a {1,} b")

bugs.summary: The debug f-string '{x=}' itself works, but the parser rejects a tuple inside an f-string field ('{1,=}'), a valid f-string construct, failing the whole file.
bugs.repro: print(f"a {1,=} b")

bugs.summary: A bare 'raise' to reraise the current exception is rejected at parse time; valid in-scope Python.
A single-element bare tuple via trailing comma (x = 1,) is rejected as a parse error; multi-element bare tuples and explicit (1,) work, so only the one-element trailing-comma form is broken.
bugs.repro:
x = 1,
print(x)

bugs.summary: del of a closed-over (nonlocal) variable does not unbind it; subsequent read returns the old value instead of raising NameError.
A generator that yields from itself via a module-global name raises NameError on that validly-bound name instead of the expected ValueError; the global binding is lost on generator re-entry.
Implicit nonlocal closure over a variable defined in the enclosing scope after the nested function raises NameError instead of capturing it.
A generator expression nested inside another generator expression triggers an internal stack underflow on valid supported syntax.
map() with multiple iterables is rejected with a TypeError; CPython zips the iterables and calls the function with multiple args.
bugs.repro: print(list(map(lambda a, b: a + b, [1, 2], [10, 20])))
bugs.actual: __VMERR__ TypeError: wrong number of arguments to builtin

bugs.summary: The '*' dynamic-width specifier in %-formatting (e.g. '%*d') is rejected as an unsupported format character on valid supported input.
Unary plus on a bool returns the bool instead of converting to int 1.
bugs.repro: print(+True)

bugs.summary: The builtin name 'slice' is bound to a constructor function rather than the slice type, so 'type(s) is slice' is False; also tuple/multi-dim subscripts like a[1:2,4:5] are parse-rejected.
str(bytes, encoding) returns the encoding string itself instead of decoding the bytes.
bugs.repro: print(str(b"123", "utf-8"))

bugs.summary: type(instance) does not compare equal or identical to its own class.
An instance attribute named __add__ blocks operator dispatch so c + 1 fails instead of using the class __add__ method (special methods should be looked up on the type).
bugs.actual: __VMERR__ TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for +: 'object' and 'int'

bugs.summary: The bitwise | operator does not fall back to the right operand's reflected __ror__ method (while __radd__/__rmul__ do work).
bugs.actual: __VMERR__ TypeError: bitwise op requires integer operands

bugs.summary: Nested del target del d[0][0] deletes the outer key instead of the inner key.
KeyError stores the missing key coerced to a string; er.args[0] is '0' (str) instead of 0 (int).
A keyword-only arg with a default that is followed by a required keyword-only arg does not get its default applied when omitted; VM raises NameError instead of using the default.
A call mixing a starred argument that is itself a function call with a keyword argument whose value is also a function call corrupts argument evaluation and raises TypeError: object is not callable.
A default-valued parameter before *args is left unbound when its default is used, raising NameError instead of using the default.
Exhausting a generator via explicit next() surfaces an uncatchable internal RuntimeError instead of a catchable StopIteration, so user except StopIteration never runs.
Iterators support next(it) but lack the explicit __next__ dunder method, raising AttributeError on supported core iterator-protocol usage.
bugs.repro: print(iter(range(4)).__next__())

bugs.summary: list += range (a supported iterable) raises TypeError even though list.extend(range(...)) works; += should accept any iterable like CPython.
bugs.repro:
l = [1, 2]
l += range(6, 10)
print(l)
bugs.actual: __VMERR__ TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for +: 'list' and 'range'

bugs.summary: Negative-step slice with out-of-bounds negative start/stop computes the wrong element range.
list.sort() does not dispatch to a user-defined __lt__ (which works for direct < comparison), raising TypeError instead of sorting.
bugs.actual: __VMERR__ TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'object' and 'object'

bugs.summary: Augmented set assignment (|= etc.) rebinds to a new set instead of mutating in place, so an aliasing identity check is False instead of True.
bugs.repro:
s1 = s2 = set('abc')
s1 |= set('ad')
print(s1 is s2, len(s1))

bugs.summary: The 'c' presentation type uses left-align by default; CPython defaults 'c' to right-align (numeric), so padding goes on the wrong side.
bugs.repro: print(">{:4c}<".format(65))

bugs.summary: For string type with leading 0 in the spec (CPython 3.10+), zero-fill should left-align and pad with '0' after the text; VM right-aligns and/or ignores the 0 fill char.
bugs.repro: print(">{:06s}<".format("ab"))

bugs.summary: '%d' % obj does not invoke the object's __int__; VM raises a format error instead of producing the converted integer.
bugs.actual: __VMERR__ ValueError: format spec requires an integer

bugs.summary: for-loop and genexpr over a generator that yields None stop immediately; ForIter treats yielded None as the exhaustion sentinel.
Two consecutive bare 'yield' statements parse-reject; a bare yield followed by another yield is rejected.
bugs.repro:
def g():
 yield
 yield
print(list(g()))
